1: The superscription designates Psalm 55 a maskil — the same genre term used for Psalms 32, 42, 44–45, 52–55, 74, 78, 88–89, and 142, typically translated "instructional poem" or "contemplation." Many commentators associate its contents with the Absalom revolt and the defection of Ahithophel, David's close counselor who joined the rebellion (2 Sam 15–17). The betrayal language of Psalms 55:12–14 and 20–21 fits that narrative precisely — a trusted companion who turned an intimate relationship into a weapon. The poem is performed with nĕgînôt (stringed instruments), placing it in formal temple or levitical musical practice.
2: "I am troubled in my lament and I am agitated" — ʾāhîmāh bĕśîḥî. The verb hāmâ (to make a noise, murmur, roar) describes the inward turbulence of distress — a churning emotional state that cannot be stilled. The same root describes the roaring of armies (Psalms 46:6), the tumult of the sea (Psalms 46:3), and the groaning of the hungry (Psalms 59:15). The psalmist's inner state matches the violence of the outer threat; the prayer is not composed but raw and urgent.
3: "The threatening voice of the enemy, the oppressive presence of the wicked" — qôl ʿôyēb (voice of the enemy) and ʿăqat rāšāʿ (the pressing/oppression of the wicked). The combination of audible threat and physical oppressive pressure captures two modes of intimidation: the verbal and the social. In a court or legal context, "the voice of the enemy" might specifically mean false accusation or slander before a tribunal — a weapon the Psalter frequently invokes (Psalms 22:8; Psalms 42:10; Psalms 43:2).
4: "My heart is writhing in anguish within me" — libbî yāḥîl bĕqirbî. The verb ḥîl describes the convulsive writhing of labor pains (Isaiah 13:8; Psalms 26:17; Jeremiah 22:23; Micah 4:10) and earthquake (Psalms 97:4); applied to the heart, it conveys not mere sadness but a violent interior upheaval. "The terrors of death have fallen on me" — ʾêmôt māwet (terrors of death): in ANE thought, the realm of death (Sheol) exerted a gravitational pull even on the living through extreme suffering; the dying person was already partly claimed by the underworld.
5: "Fear and trembling have overtaken me, and shuddering has covered me over" — the triad yirʾāh (fear), rāʿad (trembling), and pallāṣût (shuddering, horror) describes the full-body physical response to mortal threat. The phrase yirʾāh wārāʿad (fear and trembling) appears in the NT at Philippians 2:12, where Paul applies it to the moral seriousness of the believer's working out of salvation — the same language for confronting ultimate stakes. Pallāṣût (shuddering) occurs only here and at Isaiah 21:4, where the watchman is overcome with horror at the vision of Babylon's fall.
6: "If only I had wings like a dove! I would fly away and find rest" — the dove was associated with peace, purity, and swift flight in the ANE. Egyptian wisdom texts use bird-flight as an image of escape from distress. The dove is the bird of peace offerings (tôr or yônāh) and appears throughout the Psalter as a vulnerable, beloved creature (Psalms 68:13; Psalms 74:19). The wish for wings is not a fantasy of power (eagle-wings, as in Isaiah 40:31) but of quiet escape — the dove as the creature that retreats rather than fights. The wilderness (midbār) in Psalms 55:7 is not desolate wasteland but uninhabited space free from urban corruption.
7: "I would flee far away; I would make my dwelling in the wilderness" — the wilderness (midbār) in the Psalter's imagination is a place of paradox: dangerous and yet free from the specific evil of the corrupt city. The Elijah tradition (1 Kings 19:4–8) and the Mosaic tradition (Exodus 3:1) both locate divine encounter in the wilderness — a place where the noise of social oppression is absent and YHWH's voice becomes audible. The speaker's wish is not to flee God but to flee the political intrigue and betrayal that has infected the city.
8: "I would hurry to my refuge from the driving wind and the storm" — māḥôz lî (a harbor/refuge for me) combined with sûpāh (storm, tempest) and śāʿar (raging wind). The storm imagery is picked up from ANE divine-warrior theology: storms are the chariot of the divine warrior (Psalms 18:10; Psalms 29:3–9; Habakkuk 3:8). Here the speaker flees the storm rather than riding it — the contrast between the divine warrior who commands the storm and the vulnerable human who needs shelter from it.
9: "Confuse them, O Lord, split their tongues apart" — ballēʿ ʾadonāy pallag śĕpātām. The first verb ballaʿ (to swallow up, confuse, destroy) and the second pallag (to divide, split) together invoke the Babel narrative (Genesis 11:7–9), where YHWH confused the tongues of those who conspired against him. The prayer is for a Babel-like dissolution of the conspiracy: if tongues are split, coordination is broken. The violence and contention (ḥāmās wĕrîb) in the city are what make this prayer urgent.
10: "Day and night they patrol its walls; iniquity and trouble are entrenched within it" — yômam wālaylāh yĕsôbĕbûhā ʿal-ḥômōtêhā. The normal city watchman (šōmēr) guards the walls against external threat; here the watchmen are themselves the agents of wickedness. The city that should be a place of order protected by YHWH's covenant has become a place where lawlessness is the constant patrol. ʿĀwel (iniquity) and ʿāmāl (trouble, toil, misery) are its permanent residents — a biting reversal of what a covenant city should be.
11: "Ruin is entrenched at its center; oppression and fraud never leave its public spaces" — hawwôt bĕqirbāh (ruin/destruction in its interior) and tôk ûmirmāh (oppression and fraud) in the rĕḥōbôt (public squares, market spaces). In ANE city design, the rĕḥôb (plaza/square) was the site of legal proceedings, commerce, and community life — the most public space in the city. Fraud and oppression colonizing this space means the covenant functions of city life (justice, fair commerce, community protection) have been wholly subverted. The city exists as an anti-covenant space.
12: "For it is not an enemy who insults me — that I could endure; nor is it someone who hates me who has risen up against me — from that I could hide" — the rhetorical move is precise: external hostility is manageable because it is legible. The ANE code of honor assumed declared enemies — known opponents with known motives. Against such threats, one could take defensive measures or invoke legal protections. The insult of a known enemy could be answered with equal force or absorbed within the logic of public contest. It is the betrayal by the trusted insider that has no defense.
13: "But it is you — a person like me, my peer, my companion, the one I confided in" — ʾattāh ʾĕnôš kĕʿerkî ʾallûpî ûmĕyuddāʿî. Three terms of closeness: kĕʿerkî (my equal, my match — the same social standing), ʾallûp (intimate friend, one in whom confidence is placed — from the root for learning and familiarity), and mĕyuddāʿî (the one I know, my known one — a term of deep personal acquaintance). The progression moves from social equality to emotional intimacy to intellectual trust. In the Absalom narrative, Ahithophel was David's most trusted counselor; his counsel was "as if one consulted the word of God" (2 Samuel 16:23).
14: "We shared deep, sweet conversation together; together we walked in God's house among the festival crowd" — ʾăšer yaḥdāw namtîq sôd bĕbêt ʾĕlōhîm nĕhalēk bĕrāgeš. The word sôd (intimate counsel, secret conversation) is the term for the divine council in which the prophets stand (Jeremiah 23:18, 22; Amos 3:7; Psalms 89:7) — the closest possible circle of confidential relationship. To share sôd was to be admitted to the inner chamber of trust. "Festival crowd" (rāgeš — the tumultuous gathering of pilgrims) suggests the relationship was formed in the context of shared temple worship — the betrayal is thus also a desecration of that sacred bond.
15: "Let death ambush them; let them go down to Sheol alive" — the imprecation draws on the Korah narrative (Numbers 16:31–33), where the ground opened and swallowed the rebels "alive" (ḥayyîm) into Sheol — the most vivid OT depiction of divine judgment overtaking the wicked without the dignity of a normal death. The prayer is that the conspirators meet a similarly exemplary end: not quiet death but consumed by the power they were defying. Such imprecations are covenant curses invoked against covenant violators — the same logic as the treaty-curse formulas of Deuteronomy 27–28.
16: "As for me, I will call upon God, and the LORD will rescue me" — the sharp contrast (ʾănî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm ʾeqrāʾ) turns from imprecation to personal commitment. The verb qārāʾ (to call, cry out) is the Psalter's characteristic verb for prayer in extremity (Psalms 4:3; Psalms 17:6; Psalms 18:6; Psalms 22:2; Psalms 31:17; Psalms 34:6, 17). The name YHWH follows deliberately — not merely God as a general divine being but the covenant God of Israel whose personal name carries the weight of all prior acts of rescue.
17: "Evening and morning and at noon I cry out my lament and sigh; and he hears my voice" — the triple prayer rhythm (ʿereb wĕbōqer wĕṣohŏrāyim) is an early attestation of what became the fixed Jewish prayer hours: Maariv (evening), Shacharit (morning), and Mincha (afternoon/noon). Daniel 6:10 establishes the same three-daily pattern during the Babylonian exile, suggesting the custom was ancient and not exilic invention. In the ANE, marking prayer three times per day was a discipline of covenant loyalty — a daily rhythm of address to the covenant God. The pattern also echoes the divine creation of time markers (evening, morning — Gen 1) as the framework within which covenant life unfolds.
18: "He ransoms me safely from the battle I am in, for they are many who oppose me" — pādāh bĕšālôm napšî miqqĕrāb-lî. The verb pādāh (redeem, ransom) carries the economic-legal sense of paying the price to recover something held captive. Applied to divine rescue, it means YHWH pays the full cost to bring the speaker safely out of the siege — bĕšālôm (safely, in wholeness, with shalom intact) — not merely survival but restoration of the whole person. "Battle" (qĕrāb) can mean military combat but also legal contest — the full-court press of organized opposition.
19: "God will hear and afflict them, the one who is enthroned from of old" — qōšēb qedem (the one enthroned from antiquity/from of old). This divine attribute — ancient sovereignty over time — contrasts with the conspirators' momentary power. The phrase echoes the language of Psalms 29:10 (YHWH sits enthroned over the flood, forever) and Psalms 93:2 (your throne is established from of old). "Because they show no sign of change (ʾên ḥălîpôt lāhem) and have no fear of God" — the Hebrew means literally "there are no changes for them" — they are locked in an unchanging wickedness; the yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm (fear of God) that would prompt repentance is absent.
20: "He raised his hand against those who were at peace with him; he broke his covenant" — šillah yādāyw bišlōmāyw ḥillēl bĕrîtô. "Those who were at peace with him" (šĕlōmāyw) are those with whom a covenant of peace existed — the same covenant partner language used for treaties (baʿal bĕrît, "lords of covenant"). "He broke his covenant" (ḥillēl bĕrîtô) — ḥillēl means to profane, defile, or violate something sacred. A covenant had the character of a sacred oath enforced by divine witness; to break it was an act of sacrilege as much as a political offense. This is the deepest charge against the betraying companion — not merely personal treachery but covenant violation before God.
21: "His words were smoother than butter, yet war was in his heart; his speech was softer than oil, but it was all drawn swords" — the antithesis between smooth speech and violent intent is one of the most striking images in the Psalter. Māʾăsuppāyw ḥālĕqû miḥemʾāh (his words were smoother than cream/butter) and rakkû dibĕrāyw miššemen (his words were softer than oil). In ANE diplomatic language, smooth speech was the mark of court protocol and treaty negotiation; treaties were made with elaborate formal language precisely to bind parties. This smooth speaker uses treaty language while concealing "war in his heart" (ûqĕrāb libbô) — the archetype of the covenant traitor who invokes peace-language to enable attack. This verse became proverbial in the wisdom tradition for the danger of flattery (Proverbs 5:3–4; Psalms 26:23–28).
22: "Throw your burden onto the LORD, and he will hold you up" — hašlēk ʿal YHWH yĕhābĕkā wĕhûʾ yĕkalkĕlekā. The word yĕhāb (burden, what is given or assigned to one) is an Aramaic loanword appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible — its rarity suggests the verse may draw on wisdom tradition broader than the Israelite core texts. The verb hišlîk (to throw, cast) is forceful — not a gentle laying down but an active transfer of the burden's full weight onto YHWH. The concept was cited by Peter (1 Peter 5:7: "cast all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you"), though the Greek draws on a different idiom. The promise "he will never let the righteous be shaken" (yānîṭ, to totter) echoes Psalms 15:5 and Psalms 16:8 — the covenant community has a stability the wicked lack.
23: "But you, O God, will bring them down into the pit of ruin; men of bloodshed and treachery will not reach half their days" — bĕʾēr šaḥat (the pit of destruction/Sheol's well) is a common OT image for the grave and the underworld (Psalms 30:9; Psalms 103:4; Job 17:14; Psalms 33:18; Isaiah 38:17). "Not reach half their days" (lōʾ yeḥĕṣû yĕmêhem) — premature death as a divine judgment cutting off the wicked before their full allotted span. Proverbs and Deuteronomy promise long life to the righteous (Proverbs 3:2, 16; Deuteronomy 5:33) and early death to the wicked (Proverbs 10:27) — this verse applies that wisdom principle as a prayer-imprecation against the betrayer. The final clause "But I — I will trust in you" (waʾănî ʾeḇṭaḥ-bāk) is the psalm's final word, marking the transition from complaint and imprecation to renewed personal trust.
